label: positive
text: If you are considering this game you likely saw videos of the flashy combat, stylish game world, and thought that this could potentially be a fun, vast, open world that you could sink hours and hours into. It seems to be all true. But, this is also a Gacha game, so while you may hesitate to get involved with this type of genre (i was) - and not get to experience all that glorious combat and open world content- I'm going to defuse for you the powder keg that is Gacha here with two easy points to keep in mind while playing this game, so you can take advantage of all this game has to offer and also keep your wallet safe. *Point number 1. The 'free' part of the game is incredibly vast, on purpose. New characters are typically released over a period of 3 weeks. That's a long amount of time they need players to hang around to keep them as potential buyers when the next new character drops. So how do they keep players around? Give them compelling things to do. Story quests, puzzles, bosses, gear hunting, team building, lore and world building all entrenched in some really fun gameplay mechanics. It's all here. When you see the amount of events you have access to participate in when you are an appropriate level, you will know what I mean. The great thing is, everything you do gives you gems that you can throw at the Gacha system if you choose, but that leads me to my next point. *Point number 2. In terms of this Gacha system, there are really only two cash cows. 1 is you, and the other is the game world itself. If you have fun playing this game, and you mine the gameworld for all its worth in free gems, over the long term you will allow yourself to naturally pull hundreds of times on whatever character banner you want. That's hundreds of dollars worth of what some people may put into the game just to fast track obtaining a character instantly. Will you for sure get the character you try for, after saving up all those gems? No. And that's the true catch. But that catch, unfortunately, is also just a universal truth about what tends to make things more memorable. Does the raid boss in WOW always drop the piece you need? No. But when it does, you may remember that event for life, depending on how hard it was to get. So if you manage to keep the mindset that there is a years worth of game to play for you here for free, and you are not in a rush to get through it, you can have a 1-game-to-rule-them-all situation, and wallet that stays full too. So yes. The game is stylish and flashy. The combat is great. The open world, is actually jaw dropping. And the Gacha system is real. Luckily for you the Gacha system can be defeated if you simply remember to just not be in a hurry, accept what life gives you, and enjoy what's in front of you as you go.
notes: Points out many positives, and the negative of the gacha system, then tries to explain that gacha isn't bad

text: My Steam hours dont even show the whole story here since I was playing this day one from Epic... it's prolly 700+ hours deep at this point. As a day one player and a spender on the game, I really want to address the good and the bad after 2 years of playing. The Good First the good... combat. As someone who is also a day one PGR player from the same developer Kuro Games, I already knew these devs can cook action games. The combat is elite. I like the cool flashy moments, the parry mechanics, and the fact that they drop experimental features every patch to keep things fresh. It just feels fast and satisfying. The grapple hook, wall-running, and mid-air dashes make exploring the map so much smoother too since you aren't fighting a stamina bar the whole time. Visually, the game is stunning in every new region they drop. They really stepped up the map designs compared to the early areas. The music is also a banger, it actually carries the vibe during bosses and exploration. The character designs are pretty good too, altho it still pales in comparison to PGR, but when they hit, they look great. Plus, the rogue-like Illusive Realm (lezgo its back) is actually super fun to mess around in and let you draft fun builds. It's also great that we have these other methods of exploration now like wings and the bike for those who don't want to waste too much time just running around. Obligatory *devs listened*. The Bad But now the bad... it is poorly optimized. I cannot play it on my laptop anymore just after 1 year and a half, so I had to move completely to my pc now just to run it without stutters. A lot of people with high-end rigs are still complaining about stutters during long sessions, so it's clearly an engine issue they need to fix. While having those exploration tools like the wings and bike is nice, it does trivialize it a bit. The maps now basically become a big checklist of get here fast, open chest, done, move to the next. There is no real awe or wonder of finding hidden stuff anymore. Also they literally took actual flying (not gliding) with wings away since we were in a new region just to add it right back on the bike... like make up your mind. It is also touted as "Gathering Wives" since males are so rare they might as well not exist in this game. It basically becomes a harem of sort where every girl is just obsessed with you, when it is not marketed as such in the beginning. Having cool males is not gonna make their game bad. In fact it will be better so the vibe doesn't feel off, and it gives the waifu enjoyers a patch to save up or even have some other options. PGR is having the same problem but it's not as bad as WUWA. Another problem is that beginning... that was a very bad launch and it basically bled out players of the game early on. It didn't really have a compelling story and world up until 2.0... that's more than 6 months from launch, which is way too late for people who already quit. Even the storytelling now has this weird habit where they introduce a "character of the week" who gets all the attention in the main quest, only to be completely thrown to the side and forgotten the second their banner ends. It more so becomes a glorified character quest than a main one. On top of that, the endgame now feels like a massive character check with ridiculous shills for new units (sure power creep needs to be a thing for them to sell characters but still). They'll drop a paid outfit for someone random instead of the more popular beloved ones. The rerun banners make no sense. They release a new support that perfectly matches an older character, and then they just don't rerun those older ones for ages. Older units are basically getting abandoned in the meta and the story. 1.X chars are pretty much dead at this point. The echo farming is also a total nightmare. The layers of RNG with the main stats and then using tuners just to roll flat defense or HP is just a miserable experience. You can waste days of overworld grinding and get zero progression. Obligatory *K*ll Kuro games*. The Blueprint Problem Now not to be the comparing one, but yes it has borrowed a lot of things from Genshin and put some spin to it, but I wish it hasn't. I wish they just followed stuff more from PGR. Like the gacha not being a crappy 50/50. We are gated by such restrictive systems on character ascension. Why don't we have a dorm/or even a house system yet? And the skins they've released so far look kinda cheap, I wish they made higher quality ones like their other game. Back to the Good (Patch Reflections) Honestly though, the patches are what saved this game for me. Here's how my favorite updates went down by order of release: 1.1: This is where things finally started getting good. The launch felt so empty but when Jinhsi and Changli (mommy) dropped, the narrative quality just shot up. The main quest for Jinhsi and the companion story for Changli was cinematic. 1.3: Easily the best patch in the 1.x era. Hello Shorewife. Black Shores was such a huge upgrade in terms of environment and music, and the whole aesthetic was just beautiful. 2.0: Rinascita the rebirth. Going from Huanglong to this feels like a different game the first time around. Vibe was great. 2.2: This patch was amazing and it carried so hard until the end of the 2.x storyline. The character we meet here, Cartethyia, is easily one of the best written characters in the game. Her connection with the threnodian actually made me care and appreciate the lore bits. 2.5: Really good patch too, especially for how they handled Phrolova as a playable villain. Usually gachas redeem villains in the corniest ways, but the way her arc and story section ended here felt somber and fit the darker tone perfectly. 2.7: Literal cinematic Avengers moment. Seeing the Black Shores cast and the Ragunessi rolling up during the Dark Tides invasion while all the massive lore drops about Abby and the Fractsidus architecture were happening was insane. It felt like the culmination of everything we've been building toward since day one. 3.1: Prolly one of the best patches in gacha history. Gained daughter +1. The dynamic with young Aemeath was so well done, especially that hand-drawn scene where you walk with her (that end scene mannn *cries*). We went into the story thinking we were gonna save the world like always, but the daughter saved us instead. (f*ck you Aleph-1) 3.3: Mommy Hiyuki helping us save our daughter from a black hole... plus a literal Gundam mech fight with the Exostrider? Holyyyyyyy. The combat is easily one of the best on the market, but I just wish Kuro trusted their own blueprint more instead of copying the wrong homework. That said, I am incredibly hyped for the upcoming Cyberpunk collab. As someone who absolutely loved Edgerunners, seeing that aesthetic mix with WuWa's combat mechanics is an absolute dream. 7.5/10 - would still gather wives again.
notes: Wayy too long and possibly cause trouble for classification

label: positive
text: Fellow ex Genshin enjoyer. I do not regret leaving my 2000+ hours and 300+ account behind for Wuthering Waves. I played it at launch but it was rather rough, however at this date I just cant wrap my head around how anyone would choose Genshin Impact over it. I suppose you can enjoy both at the same time, but Wuwa removes every single thing I hated about Genshin. Usually I dont do reviews like this, but heres a nice list of pros: -Sprinting does not consume stamina outside of combat -You can run up walls instead of slowly climbing them -Dialogue can be skipped -You can farm Echos (Wuwas Artifact counterpart) at any time, be it standard enemies or bosses, even if you are out of daily energy. However, EXP mats are another story... -Actual combat that requires skill instead of just premium Elemental Mastery teams -Bosses respawn immediately -No stupid weekly material rotations -You dont need 5 million different materials for one character, they use the same for everything, depending on weapon class -You dont have to teleport around like an idiot to craft things or complete dailies -The game tries different camera angles, and doesnt have the same two NPC models with only 5 animations -NO PAIMON, free from this aneurysm inducing little twerp who has to comment on, and recap every single thing -Weapon banners are guaranteed to give you the event weapon, you read that right! -Developers actually listen to the community (even outside of China) Tho unfortunately the lack of creativity map wise is a bit evident. While I really like the new areas, and while they are creative on their own, its not hard to see how they were heavily inspired by Fontaine and Natlan at their core... yall can do better on that part. (But I guess Septimont is what Natlan was supposed to be, so its better in the end?) Otherwise the best gacha/BOTW clone/ARPG I ever played. Im pretty confident even people outside of the gacha sphere will love this game.

label: positive
text: Honestly this was a big surprise. I decided to play it just to have some downtime from fps games. A game I wanted to play just because of the 'flight' utility; see the world from above and enjoy the scenery. I did the "viral" global filter from tiktok too, to make it look more story book like (high contrast and color while lowering brightness). And to my surprise it was one of the few game that that I would find soothing and relaxing: more often than not I would pass out mid convo with NPCs. But after some time into it and going through some key story events, the whole world opens its doors wide. The maps are beautiful, with each region having distinct architecture and views that are just simply breathtaking. And other than the Main story events there are those centered around the characters/companion (companion quests). Though some are boring, I am glad to say that at the time of writing/typing, there were only two that I found below average in terms to enjoyment. Most of them are enjoyable, and at times I would actually be emotionally invested (to the point of tears) for certain companion quests. Of which was, in my case, quite rare: especially from Gatcha games. And before I knew it, I was invested. Currently I now play for the immersion and to see the great animation and visuals that WuWa has to offer. And I tell you, the rendered animations scenes can be crazy good. As a result of this, I'm now actively trying to force myself to play fps so that I don't binge too much on the quests and story in WuWa. Other than thaaat: Grinding is not much of hassle and is quite simple thanks to tons of QoL features. Gameplay is fun and easy to get into but can be quite challenging at times. As for the characters, though some are quite frankly meh or alright, there are quite a lot of them that are beautifully designed with personalities that can leave quite an impression on you. In conclusion, try it out it might impress you! besides.. it's free anyway!! :)

label: positive
text: In a genre that can be predatory towards consumers, Wuthering Waves is in a league of it's own (with a couple caveats). I finished the story, side quests, and about 98% of the exploration currently available and these are my observations so far. Positives: -Combat is fluid and satisfying. -There's almost always something to work towards upgrading on your characters. -Character upgrades feel substantial. -Exploration feels rewarding for the vast majority of the game. -Most everything is documented in game. Tutorials, guides, where to get the item you're looking for, etc... -Mostly excellent voice acting. -Excellent music (look up "In Moments Does Moon Persist (Iuno Theme)") -Non pushy monetization system. -you can guarantee yourself the characters or weapons you want on the banners if you're smart with your pull currency. Neutral: -Fun puzzles, but most are remedial. I would like optional harder puzzles for further rewards. -The story is epic, touching, and very well written at times, but drags on sometimes. I skipped a lot. -Characters are not created equal. Some are good, some are bad, regardless of skill. -You're going to be in menus a ton. It pays off but it's cumbersome. Negative: -Co-op is not good. Most of what I wanted to do with my friend was blocked by the game as single player only. Not Sure: -Having finished the story and exploration content, my opportunities for easy pull currency are limited to events, dailies, weeklies and monthlies. Apart from that I can wait for new patch content. I would imagine growth will slow significantly, but I've only just made it here. I do worry that this is the point that people start pulling out the credit card and get into trouble. I haven't spent money on the game yet but I do plan to when something catches my eye. For the 135 hours the game has given me so far it deserves some cash. Most of the micro-transactions seem not worth it, but when a cosmetic i want or something cool comes around I won't hesitate to buy it. Anyways, the game is free. if it sounds like your thing, try it out! It's good fun, just be aware of the gacha genre and resist gambling your irl money away unless you are in a position to do so without repercussions.

text: Strictly reviewing as a former Genshin player comparing it to Genshin for those who are interested. TLDR: it pretty much beats Genshin in every single aspect in my opinion, except for music. Here are the specific things for those who are considering jumping ship from Genshin: 1. Teleportation to important events as a new player do not require manual teleportation unlock. It doesn't try to waste your time with forced "map exploration". 2. Character gacha is more lenient (80 rolls for pity at 50/50), Weapon gacha is guaranteed at 80. Soft pity is noticeable once you are at 50+ rolls. 3. Daily stamina (the thing you use to consume for time-locked grinding) has a bank for regeneration overflow. Most dungeons also give you the option of x2 consumption without the need to craft any items (one grinding run counts twice if you spend double stamina). It's completely free and a built-in feature without you having to do anything extra. 4. Echos (artifact eqivalent) can drop from normal enemies at top rarities. Also after a certain point, all echo drops are 5 star at 100% chance. You will never have those useless purple artifacts in Genshin. 5. Open world items collection for character limit breaks are entirely guided by the UI. It shows you exactly where things are and they require far fewer in quantity than Genshin. One day's collection run can get you through most of what you want to do (though I think Genshin more recently implemented something similar?). 6. Combat is more action based and character controls are actually important. You can solo your favorite character if you truly want to. No character is truly a backstage "support" in Genshin whose only purpose is to give buffs. 7. Manual input and enemy reading determines combat, not superficial knowledge checks about elemental reactions like in Genshin. It's not like Genshin's 4-character braindead swap circles for elemental reactions (splash, swap, splash, swap, splash, swap, boom ad nauseam). 8. Dialogues can be skipped entirely. 9. Daily mission is just 1 and it covers most of your daily reward. It's very time friendly. It usually involves talking to 1-2 NPC or killing 1-3 enemies. Symbolic and respects your time. 10. Climbing has the "running" option. 11. Normal running doesn't consume stamina. No open-world stamina annoyance. 12. Quest markers are far more accurate than in Genshin. 13. Your character can literally fly like a jet plane in addition to the usual Genshin gliding, giving you Z-axis. It gives you more creative ways to access the map without wasting your time. 14. As a new player, you will mostly get a 5 star character you want because there are several guaranteed banners exclusively for new players, some characters are not even supposed to be available on your current banners. I started off with a past-event character I really like that isn't on the current event banner exclusive to me as a new player. You get that for weapons as well. 15. Additionally there are fixed common pool 5 star characters that appear in new player events. The freedom of selection is fairly high so you are pretty much guaranteed a full team of 5 stars within your first few days. 16. Enemy drops do not require manual pickup. 17. Chests on the map can be clearly detected by the UI once you unlock the features. 18. There is no weekday rotation for any kind of farming. All farming is available at all times. 19. Characters are built VERY differently from each other. Button smashing wouldn't do you any good. You actually have to learn the input and combos. 20. Built in guides and recommendations from the game client itself (though I think Genshin added this as well). It also has character-specific tutorial to walk you through the specific mechanics and input/combos. Now for the more opinion based comments: 1. The music is inferior to that of Genshin, mostly because they lack distinct melodies and feel truly like "generic video game background noises". 2. The character designs are superior to Genshin, but I understand this depends on your demographic. There are far fewer childish characters here and most look and act sexy/handsome/mature. And I don't mean lolis. I am mostly talking about characters who act and look one dimensional like a Disney character regardless of body type. Plenty of those in Genshin. 3. The story is less childish. This isn't a fault of Genshin as Genshin still targets children for a lot of its contents. This one has far less silliness even in side quests. There are not that many sanitized optional contents with cringeworthy pixar/disney style side character plots with all sunshine and rainbows that torture adult players. 4. The main character speaks and is part of the story. The silly and childish "banter" is a lot more toned down than the MC+Paimon combo in Genshin. It is far less aggravating. 5. Cooking so far seems entirely optional as it wasn't required by any quest I encountered. I prefer this to the heavy-handed push for cooking in Genshin.

label: positive
text: CCP Gotcha Game with Kernel level anti cheat that's practically spyware. I can't see anything wrong with the game so far regarding unauthorized programs being run like with Once Human but can you really risk it? This is something you'll have to answer for yourself. I've run CMD scripts and verified for myself that 1) The ACE program does not stay active after the game closes out. 2) ACE does fully delete upon deletion of the game. I'm just starting to play this game but I'd thought I'd comment on it seeing this was two of the biggest concerns about Chinese based games. Update: Cheating I've created several accounts to test the ACE anti-cheat program. I've poked and prodded. I've used 3 different injection methods and every combination of cheats there is. Meaning, activating and deactivating different cheats for different parts of the game to decrease the chances of detection. What I found was that the code injectors were detected INSTANTLY every time. I have two issues with the enforcement of anti-cheat policies with this game. First, there is no live banning. If you're going to cheat you will be able to do so for the entire duration of your session so long as you do not log out. Why is this a problem? Creators of cheat trainers can promote the sale of their programs by showing you live videos of them spending hours in the game "undetected" when in fact, the cheats have been detected and their account has already been banned. Finally, you're able to create accounts based on Temp-email generators (Throw away email accounts). This allows for the creation of infinite accounts subsequently bypassing any means to enforce a ban. While it will require you to start from the beginning, the point is you will still be able to play. This also works if you switch your existing banned account to a new server, but once again you will be starting from the beginning. Do cheats and trainers work in this game? Absolutely! However, anyone telling you it's undetectable and/or won't result in a ban, or if you follow certain steps, or activate and deactivate certain cheats at certain times will prevent detection is 100% lying to you. The ACE program detects script injections instantly. What kind of ban are we talking about here? Well, let me put it to you this way. All my accounts are 3 days into a 24hr ban. So, I'm going to go out on a limb here and say perma ban. Now to play this game in earnest and give my thoughts on it. Update: Sorry, but I had to turn off comments. I was tired of manually deleting all the drive-by posting. I almost forgot how petty people can be. Final Update: I'll put my overall impression first so you don't have to read a wall of text. Wuthering Waves is a standout in its genre for its combat depth, movement mechanics, and generous gacha system. If you enjoy action-oriented RPGs with skill-based combat and exploration, it's very compelling. There is plenty of content in the game, it's fun, and it's free. I promise, it is worth at least 300hrs of fun. Double that if you follow the story and don't skip cut scenes. The game is not dependent on gatcha (convene) pulls and the gotcha system does not prevent you from doing literally anything in the game. Each character is a genuine addition to your collection with unique combat animations, styles, and personalities. However, it's not without its flaws. Storytelling is an absolute mess, the story itself is all over the place and makes literally no sense at all. Technical issues remain (especially on weaker hardware), and some of the gacha and side-content design may feel repetitive or frustrating. The music is pure cringe at times, not all the time though, the mouth movements on characters are not synced and at times don't move at all, and the English voice acting is very bad. Simple Chinese words we know how to pronounce in English are butchered leading many to suspect AI based translations. There is an English voice acting team for certain but who knows in these crazy times. Finally, once you've made it to the end, that's it. Once you've done every main mission, side mission, puzzle, and event there's no reason to continue playing until new content with new character releases. Bottom line: It's a very good, very enjoyable game, and many of its strengths outweigh its drawbacks but whether it's worth it will depend a lot on what kind of player you are. Gotcha games tend to rely on character collection rather than dynamic storytelling. Read below for my more in-depth analysis. Pros: Dynamic, High-Skill Combat: Combat is very engaging: You have parries, dodges, combos, and character intro/outro skills that reward timing and strategy. Mastering character synergy (via team swapping) is satisfying and deep. Exploration & Movement Mechanics: The world design encourages vertical movement: wall-running, grappling hooks, and other traversal mechanics make exploration feel fluid and fun. Because of these mobility tools, exploration feels more dynamic than in many open-world gacha games. Generous Gacha Economy: Theres a pity system (hard pity for 5-star Convenes) that is more forgiving than some other gacha games. The game provides relatively generous free rewards, making it more accessible to free-to-play players. Echo System: Instead of just regular gear, you equip Echoes (monster-like companions) that boost stats or give unique effects, adding strategic depth. Echoes are farmable without strict daily limits, which avoids some gacha fatigue. Strong Visual & Audio Presentation: The games art direction leans into a moody, cinematic anime realism, which helps build a compelling, atmospheric world. Character animations especially during combat are polished and expressive. Meaningful Progression & Reward Systems: The progression systems (leveling, Echoes, character tuning) offer depth without overwhelming newer players. Kuro Games has shown commitment to compensating players when there are issues (bugs, launches, etc.). Cons: Technical / Performance Issues: On lower or mid tier rigs, performance can be inconsistent: frame drops and optimization problems are reported regularly. At launch, there were some persistent bugs, crashes, and even audio problems. Most of it has been fixed but some still persist. Narrative & Story Problems: The early parts of the story are heavy on exposition, jargon, and unclear terminology, which can be off-putting. I found the storytelling uneven, with certain character arcs or lore being too cryptic. The world mechanics are never explained. The English voice acting is flat and poorly directed. Kind of sounds like AI voice modulation at time. Element System Is Under-whelming: Although there are multiple elemental types, Their impact is shallow. For example, fire doesnt do damage over time, ice doesnt immobilize, etc. Because of that, character differentiation leans more on movement and skill combos. Repetitive / Weak Side Content: Side quests are sometimes repetitive or fetch-quest heavy. After a while, the reward system for Echoes or exploration can feel grindy or same-y. Gacha Drawbacks (Especially for Characters) While the pity system helps, character gacha can still feel punishing: standard 50/50 mechanics and very low rates for 5-star. Because of this, building a strong, optimal roster might require a lot of pulls, which can be outright infuriating for F2P. Co-op Mode Limitations: Co-op feels somewhat limited. There's little meaningful content to do together, and players sometimes feel like they're just along for the ride. Some bugs in co-op have been reported (e.g., desync, unresponsive states) that hamper the experience. Longevity / Live-Service Risk As a live-service, gacha-based game, theres always a risk of diminishing player base or less support down the line. This game very little replay value and end game burnout happens very quickly.
notes: Very long, took a while to realize this was a positive review?

text: Wuthering Waves actually managed to make me quit Genshin completely. Everything else feels so much better than Genshin, even though I have to admit the story at the beginning (Huanglong region) is bad that I think you should just skip it. Black Shore is where the story starts to become good and interesting for me, and my favorite romance part of this game is when Rover tries to save Shorekeeper. I feel like Rinascita region is where this game truly shines and the story really begins for me. And the fact that I can experience a AAA game for free is amazing. text: [h1] Definitely Worth Playing Gacha Game [/h1] Wuthering Waves is easily one of the best action-focused gacha games out right now. The combat is insanely smooth, responsive, and genuinely skill-based dodges, parries, and combo swaps feel so satisfying that even normal enemies are fun to fight. The music fits perfectly too, adding atmosphere whether youre gliding through a valley or fighting a boss. Characters? Gorgeous. label: positive
text: Despite Wuthering Waves being a gacha game, this never holds it back. The gameplay, characters, world, exploration, and music are of extremely high quality, and with every update, it delivers richer and more dynamic features that further immerse you in this innovative world. Personally, I play this game for the story and characters. I have absolutely fallen in love with the narrative Kuro is trying to convey. It is undoubtedly one of the most visually stunning, thematically rich, and emotionally resonant stories Ive encountered in a video game. It offers everything that matters to me in a story and so much more. This game has an overwhelming number of qualities that I couldn't do justice to in just a brief review. Rover feels authentic, natural, progressive, flawed, and grounded. While some might say this story is about "gathering" wives, that couldn't be further from the truth. Rover's thematic journey revolves around fate versus free will. Can they unite humanity and overcome the fated end that the Lament poses? There are many deep and intricate layers to their character, how they tie in with the world itself, the people they save, and the overarching narrative. On top of Rover, the antagonists in this story are some of the most metafictional, creative, well-written, and engaging characters Ive ever seen. They consistently deliver in terms of presentation, which only makes you more invested. They challenge Rover in a way that makes you question the choices they've made throughout their journey, as well as the core values of the world and humanity itself. I won't spoil who they are or what they do. I simply urge you to find out for yourself. The side characters are also fantastic. Especially from Rinascita (2.0) onwards, they are all brimming with life. The 1.0 story and its characters are still great, but the storytelling is weaker, which has led to less investment from people, and I completely understand that. However, every side character in 2.0 onwards has amazing dynamics with Rover. They genuinely feel like their own individuals, with emotionally cathartic backstories, realistic ideals, and world views. While some of the female characters have romance, it never detracts from their individuality. They are not reduced to mere accessories - they have their own ambitions, motivations, and ideals separate from Rover. Kuro makes an active effort to demonstrate this, and they have accomplished it fantastically. Gameplay-wise, it only complements the story being told. It becomes deeper with each update, the character kits become more complex, and they continuously add unique and engaging mechanics that breathe EVEN more life into the system, enhancing its incredible addictiveness. The exploration throughout the game is amazing and serves to connect the world while building upon it. The only gripe I have so far is in the later regions, where it is admittedly quite hand-holdy. Other than that, the game is stunning, rich, and keeps you unlocking quests, hidden secrets, and amazing lore that's scattered across this vast world. In terms of the Gacha system in this game, it is relatively fair. I've managed to pull every character I've wanted, and they are quite generous with the amount of pulls you receive as a F2P player. The best thing about the Gacha system is that the weapon banners are guaranteed. This means that when you reach 80 pulls (pity), you are guaranteed to receive the weapon of your choice. Unfortunately, characters still have a pity system, but it's a lot less taxing when you don't have to worry about losing your 50/50 on BOTH the character and weapon banners. Whilst there is still a bit of grinding, I wouldn't say it's tedious if you're a casual player. Especially since the in-game rewards system for levelling is generous, so you earn lot of pulls from simply progressing the main story. All in all, this is absolutely a F2P game worth getting invested in. Kuro games consistently makes the game better, listens to player feedback, innovates, improves the world, gameplay, and narrative. If you love anime or manga series like Bleach, Re:Zero, Steins;Gate, Cyberpunk (Edgerunners/2077), or even if you simply enjoy romance stories, I think you'll definitely find some value in this game.

text: [h2]People's Choice for 2025 for a VERY GOOD REASON.[/h2] Live service makes it tricky to navigate the balance between game dev direction and community feedback. Most devs will put up the facade of listening: social media interaction, quality localisation, surveys and questionnaires where you ask for QoL that devs may or may not put into their other games instead. But KURO GAMES [i]listens[/i]. At launch the game was admittedly a mess, with bugs, awkward localisation, and story that was all over the place. The combat and gameplay were satisfying and the visuals (environment and character design) were great and this was enough to win over a good number of loyal fans, but instead of making excuses for a messy launch, KURO locked in and has been improving steadily and quite rapidly to make the game the way it is now. The game respects your time: gacha dailies takes 5-10 mins, character building is efficient and can be done shortly after getting the shiny new waifu or husbando (in-game resources will also point you in the right direction for characters not released yet), and there are exploration tools available for finding rewards across the map. A skip button for story is provided too, though you'll rarely want to use it. But when you want to sit down and take the time to enjoy, WuWa will delight you with its gorgeous music, memorable characters, stunning environments, and engaging story. I'd like to say that no matter what your gacha history is, give WuWa a try. But ratherno matter what your gaming history is, give WuWa a try, and enjoy!

The graphics are breathtaking and the combat feels even more fluid. In my opinion, Wuthering Waves delivers one of the best action-combat systems in this genre. text: It's confusing. This is now my second time editing this review, as I just entered the 3.0 version of the story. This game is incredibly diverse to the point that at times it can feel overwhelming. Maybe I've just rushed through the game, but with my hours on record that would be very surprising. I'll try to guide you, the review reader, into what your experience with this game might be: As you'll start playing, if you've ever played any other 3d gachas, WuWa will feel very generic. I think this is actually a strong point for anyone new to 3d gachas, since most others except GI will probably expect you to have knowledge on these kinds of games already. The first 10-15 hours of the story are VERY bad. I would put it as the prologue to the prologue to the prologue. Every major version likes to flip the story on its head and introduce 30 new things that break your perception of the story up to that point. Anyway, the ending of the 1.0 story is very strong, so I urge you to get through it anyway. I think the early story is as boring as it is in order to get you interested in the game mechanically rather than narratively, but what do I know? The early exploration reminds me very much of genshin's mondstadt exploration, but with far more combat mechanics sprinkled into the mix. Luckily, KURO realized genshin's model isn't particularly fun for people more interested in anything but exploration, and so you get access to wings very early on. Early exploration is mediocre, but it gets so much better later on that I can't fault it in any way. Assuming you get past all that, let's talk about the RPG and time-gating mechanics. You have 4 major ways to improve a character: - Their level - Their weapon/weapon level - Echoes - Forte upgrades In my experience, I never once lacked money or XP things for weapons or resonators, so you're not really time-gated in any regard for those. The weapon gacha is rather generous, and if you pull a 5 star weapon then you are guaranteed to get that banner's 5 star weapon, and almost every resonator has a 4 star weapon that performs 80% as good or more. Echoes are basically the equivalent of relics from GI, but you can get them in ways that are not time-gated, such as just fighting the respective echo boss (you do not need to also claim it's rewards with the time-gated currency in order to get the echo itself). Unfortunately, it also copies the GI's relic system's substats mechanic. These are randomized substats you get at 5 level intervals on echoes. This is probably where you'll be spending most of your time in order to min-max characters. Forte upgrades only take up upgrade materials (which are very easy to obtain) up until 6/10 upgrades, where you will then need a major boss material that you can only fight 3 of a week. The amount you obtain goes up with your world level, and by the time you reach the point that you can upgrade past 6/10 you can get roughly 5-6 a week. At the point I'm at, I can get 12 a week, which means I can max out the important fortes of 1 character per week. This means you can very easily catch up all your characters to max. Now onto the endgame: what will you actually be doing once you've done everything there is? Both primary endgame gamemodes require 2 teams. I am only using old/standard resonators and have been able to complete the non-extreme parts of both of these gamemodes, so I wouldn't put their difficulty that high. Both of these gamemodes have an extra very difficult tidbit that you can optionally do for a little extra gacha currency, but I'll be honest, you do need the meta in order to clear that stuff. Unless you're 100%-ing every version as you're playing, you will have a large backlog of events and such to complete. I've only done the occasional event here and there as I've played through the story, and I'm at over 160 hours, so there is a LOT of content. All of these events give gacha currency, so it's not like you'll be doing them without an incentive. The community is also something to consider when discussing endgame, as it's probably a major factor in whether some people stick with a game or not. WuWa is much smaller than other gacha games, which makes pockets of cool people rather easy to find. I've been enjoying talking to people about the game thus far, but I can't speak for everyone's experience. Now that I've discussed the most important parts, I'll talk about what I like the most about this game: The music of this game is very good. Starting in 2.2 or so, the music of WuWa gets a huge step up in quality, to the point where you could easily make a whole playlist out of it. When I say that the music is good I don't just mean it is impressive in some specific regard such as vocals, arrangement, or something else. The music fits the atmosphere, builds on top of your experience, and is subtle enough to not steal the spotlight of what it is enhancing. There is SO MUCH LORE! I don't think it is on the level of something like Arknights (1m+ words lmao), but if you've ever played HSR and gone through the books and enemy entries in Jarilo VI, you'll enjoy this game very much. From what I can tell, most of the community completely ignores these aspects, but they're still being included in every new game version. The writers are very dedicated to the lore of this world, and I respect that very much. The combat is... something that exists, truly. I've decided to look at some endgame players and how they play the game, and it has completely broken my perception of how you're meant to play WuWa. All I'll say is that there really is no limit to what your rotation can be if you're dedicated enough. The game's performance is incredible. I will fight a war against anyone that says otherwise. I recently had to go on a week-long trip with a 7 year old laptop that has no GPU and only 8gb of ram, and that thing managed to run WuWa flawlessly 30fps. If that piece of junk ran something as "demanding" as WuWa, literally any desktop computer will too. Please, use the graphics options in the game. If it is not running smoothly, just turn some things down. Your experience isn't tied to how pretty the pixels look. And lastly, characters aren't as forgotten as I thought they would be. I'm very happy to say that KURO is putting a lot of effort to not leave old characters completely forgotten. AFAIK while there's no such thing as an old character becoming a major character again, they still make appearances, which does bring a smile to my face. WuWa is worth a try. You can be as invested as you want to and still get enjoyment out of this game. It is accessible to both absolute casuals and tryhards in every aspect of the game.

Performance can be a tiny bit questionable at times but they seem to be working on it. Tons of QOL stuff to make the gameplay experience feel better and smoother almost every patch. Overall, a solid FREE story game if you completely want to avoid the gacha aspect and only want to focus on the narrative which is quite vast by now due to all the story content that has been added till now. But if you do stick with it, the game does reward the player well enough to secure limited characters at least every other patch if not every patch. So, in conclusion, give it a try and it might just impress you!

label: positive
text: Having completed the main story up until the last area patch I think it's about time we do a lil mini review on the game overall. My biggest take away from this this game is the overall generosity it has when it comes to the gatcha mechanics of the game. I've played a handful of games with gatcha mechanics like Game of Dice, Battle Cats, Summoner War: Sky Arena, Genshin Impact and Honkia: Star Rail, but of all of them this game has shown to me it has the fairest mechanics awarding players more 4/5-stars. Although there can be an arguments that the gacha mechanics in Battle Cats is for show, mainly because almost all the cats you pull are useful in their own, unique ways, I still stand by my last statement. In the amount of hours I have put into the game just casually playing (not having spent a dime just yet), I was able to pull almost all the 5-star basic characters, almost every 4-star character and was able to obtain a few more 5-stars, including one of the current banner characters. Comparing it to Summoners War or Game of Dice is like night and day. In those games you are more likely to continue pulling the same characters/cards with little variety, but still it's much more forgiving than than either Genshin or Honkia:Star Rail if you dont mind spending less than USD 15. The later games require you to put so much more effort, time and money into the games just to obtain a chance *MAYBE* receiving that treasured 5-star, that will eventually be power crept or become useless in comparison to the new pulls. Another plus of this game is the integrated help when it comes to building your characters in the menus. In the main character screen, there is a tool that can help auto-equip the best suited items for a character, putting the highest synergy items first, then substituting if you dont have the best suited ones. There is another menu within the game that also allows you to see what pairs well with who when it comes to weapons, stat-upgrades and such. There is also a various tools online that can help you build characters, which can continue to state relevant for quite a while. Which is a relief since in the Hoyo Franchise you are expected to upkeep on a daily basis since each new patch can cause your current team to be damn-near weak/useless. The simplicity of also being taught how each character works and being rewarded for it with relevant materials for them is a bonus. When it comes to the game play, I find it much more engaging than Hoyo games and Summoner War (if you dont include PvP). In this game, it shows that the studio prides itself in being able to hook players and engage them with enemies, the environment and exploration. While trying to 100% an area can become tedious or repetitive, it continue to do well in evolving mechanics to ensure it does not become too boring and/or overwhelming. This is best displayed when you compare Huanglong and Rinascita. While finding chests became an eyesore in the first area, having access only to the lootmap to give you a general idea of where chest may be hidden, which at times can become frustrating, the new area improved on in by allowing you the ability to search for them with the Sensor ability and marking a radius where they may be located. Although this is a mechanic that has existed prior, it was a welcome addition to also add certain location radiuses for exploration ease. There was also a wide variety of exploration addition in the second area that helped to spice things up and keep them fresh like new puzzles, interactables and much more. There has also been some major improvements when it came to the story telling of the areas, with the first few areas having some lack-luster stories, as the game progressed they were able to find their 'groove' and create both narratively compelling and cohesive storylines. There still is one 5-star who has not been discussed and that is a slight gripe of mine, but hopefully he is brought up in the next upcoming patch or in the new nation of the New Federation. But besides that I find it all compelling and able to hook me for hours to look into the lore, small details and connections to characters, places and history. Also props to the team for making Abby an actual companion who can hold their own. In Genshin Paimon feels more like a plot-protected character, serving little to no main purpose besides being our somewhat-guide through places, while in this game Abby serves as the protagonist's companion and main component of Rover's abilities. Although it is still in its early stages, I have come to appreciate this game, its generosity and fairness, and its 'polish' that is noticiable in each update/patch. A must play game that is well worth your time, and money if you wish to spend, and feels more geared towards casual players with its wide implementations of 'permament' events leaving less players with FOMO. (cough* Genshin *cough)

label: positive
text: Full Game Review Been playing this game since release, long before it came to steam. My absolute playtime should be at 1200 - 1500 hours plus-minus. I have completed all of exploration, all (available) achievements, all content as a whole as a complete free to play player, so the review will be based on a F2P standpoint. Short Form: Story: 10/10 Gameplay and Progression: 8/10 Combat: 9/10 Content: 8/10 Characters: 8/10 Powercreep: 8/10 Developers: 10/10 F2P Friendliness: 7/10 Total: 8.5/10 - Definitely recommended, even if you never played a game of this genre before. Long Form: The story is one of, if not the best story I've ever seen in any Gacha game. Combine story and gameplay, and you essentially don't have a Gacha game anymore - You have a triple A game with Gacha aspects in it. It's honestly better than many recent anime, movies, video games and other series out there. There's drama, plot twists and... let's call it "romance", even though its the stereotypical harem-simulator these games were created as. That's the only negative point I can say about the story. The pacing is great and doesn't take you 6+ hours to complete after every single patch (looking at other games in this genre, you know which ones I mean), and even if they did, at least they're fully voiced and not infested by blackscreens with text every other minute. They feel interactive and immersive, and it almost never goes off-track just to raise your online time to look good for investors. The devs really value your time, which will become appearant in other sectors as well. Not only does the story get better and better with every patch, but so does the overall world building. I can't remember when the last time was that I was completely captivated by a damn side quest that explained some ancient or recent history about a location you don't really care about during the main story of a game, or the last time a note "from the past" made me upset/teary after reading it. I could go on and on about the story of this game, but a review shouldn't spoil a single part of it in my eyes. See for yourself. It's ridiculous if you compare it to other games in this genre, and it does more than just hold up if you compare it to AAA titles or even other media. This game features some of the most user friendly features I have ever seen in any game in over 20 years worth of gaming. Actual fun and fast traversal, including flying across the map, or driving customizable vehicles in the latest installment of the game. Hundreds of shortcuts for frequently used features so you don't need to search them within the UI every time you need them, and if there is no shortcut yet, you can be sure they'll add one soon. Very smooth, streamlined and fast exploration. Interactive maps like other games require? Not needed, the game essentially has it built in for most of the original map, and for basically the entirety of any map that came after 1.X. Exploration doesn't drain you out as fast as it does in other games, especially since you automatically pair it with resource and "gear" farming while you're at it, which means it's even more rewarding than it first appears to be. It's fast paced, fluid, and never feels out of place. For my taste, it could be a tiny bit faster even, but given that it's ment to be a Gacha game, this is already plenty. The world is post-apocalyptic and just feels believable. It's absolutely immersive and the story keeps tying perfectly into it, which is something I didn't expect when I first picked it up. Now, why did I have to deduct 2 points? It's the initial progression system. Once you're leveled up and able to reach max level gear easily, everything is fine. You gain resources quickly, you can build characters from zero to 100 within a day or two if you really want to invest a lot into them, and so on. However, BEFORE you reach that point, things go awfully slow. You have your story and side quest progression, your usual energy consumption which you shouldn't boost to save your energy reserves until you can effectively build characters, and exploration. Do all of them, and you still won't reach the point at which you can freely invest into whatever you want to invest into. It will take you weeks of active playing, if not 2 months or so. Maybe even longer unless you have a ton of time on your plate. Until then, not only is building characters tedious - It's straight up impossible sometimes, especially if you're preparing more than 1 team, which we'll get into when we get to content. Everything you need - Money, exp materials, talent materials etc - feels extremely scarces before you already have at least 2 fully built teams at your disposal, which you wont have until you reach said checkpoint. The big star of this game. Fast paced, real time combat that relies on personal skill rather than on the characters you own for the most part. Dodging, parying, counter attacks, tune break, hot swaps - The game took the best features of other games of its genre and made them work together. The teams are locked to 3 characters per team, which honestly makes it alot more dynamic that the "industry standard" at 4. Less characters per team means more freedom in building multiple teams, less resources per team needed, and more time efficiency overall. It also helps a ton whenever you have to learn a new rotation for a specific team. I remember that, at the start, some people compared it to souls games. Honestly, that's far from the truth, but it IS definitely one of the harder combat oriented Gacha games out there. This is where it shows one of the main differences to Genshin. WuWa was made for more skill oriented, hardcore players, whereas Genshin focuses on casual players. BUT: Here's the fun part about WuWa. You DON'T have to follow a "meta" in order to beat the game. You can essentially play any character you want to play, as long as their names aren't Yuanwu or Lingyang. You don't even have to learn standard rotations if you don't want to. WuWa allows you absolute freedom, as long as you take your time to understand its mechanics. 4-Star characters from 1.X can STILL clear content, even the MC is very damn valuable either as a DPS, Sub-DPS or Support depending on the role you want to play them as. More on that in the content section. Either way, for the more casual players, there's the previously mentioned standard rotations. You know, support, into sub-dps, into main dps. Easy, simple, efficient, and more than sufficient. For the people who want to swim away from their keyboards after sweating too much, there's a more skill based rotation form called "quickswap", which essentially allows you to have all 3 characters on the field at the same time in some situations, and requires perfect timing and understanding of the mechanics to pull off efficiently. WuWa keeps adding new events, many of which of permanent nature, that feature gameplay systems from other popular franchises. We, for example, have Tower Defense in WuWa. We have these weird zombie hoard mobile games you always get ads of. We have a card game that actually doesn't suck, and they let us abuse overpowered BS in it instead of patching it out after we find it. We have fishing that actually is not boring, or at least not as boring as it can be as shown in other titles. We have a rogue-like mode. Enough said. Endgame? I, as a F2P, clear them all with full rewards. The latest one... Missing the 2 "whale" rewards, but i get full Asterites nonetheless. You can get any characters you want as F2P, as long as you don't decide you want every single damn character there is. You need dedication and time, but you CAN pull this off. I own every premium character except for 4, which I don't even want. Powercreep? Only in numbers, not in potential. Conclusion: Give it a try. You won't be disappointed by possibly the best game in its Genre. PS: Only 5 minutes here since I play on launcher, not Steam.

label: positive
text: This is quite a first to me that I do review a gacha game or rather in general, playing one for long enough to actually judge it's quality. After my DNA (Duet Night Abyss) review earlier. I was curious enough to look at how other but similar games do and therefore decided to check out Wuthering Waves to learn more about such games in order to set up expectations but also be more familiar with the genre in case I end up reviewing more games with the same direction in the future. Before the review starts, Please note that I wouldn't be mentioning monetization or the gacha system itself as I want to review WuWa as a game itself. Wuthering Waves (WuWa) is a game that was released for the first time in May 2024. since it's launch, WuWa went through a large amount of updates and overhauls. from the questionable 1.0 release to pretty much competing with the titans in it's genre and winning awards. The story of the game does start slowly with relatively low stakes as most eastern RPG games as the focus is on world-building and getting the player to know the core basics of the game. If I had to compare it to a different then a large part of the 1.x story is very akin to FFXIV: A Realm Reborn. Once you do reach 1.3 then WuWa has established it's story and the stakes do raise up to be on par with Endwalker which also remains consistent with proper balancing between different parts of the story being told. My personal favorite was basically the story during the 1.3-2.7 patches as WuWa has managed to let me experience Endwalker once again but through a different way and also gameplay. Combat? My expectations were at first were low as most gacha games are targeting mobile devices as their main audience. However, I was pleasantly surprised to see that it wasn't the case. The combat does look simple but it also has depth as it includes parry, dodging, echo skills and also character switching which can be used for timing ultimate abilities. that alone shows that you can create a good experience for both types of players Graphics and optimization? WuWa looks great visually and is using a custom build of the Unreal Engine 4 engine. the game went for an anime style just like others within the same genre it is coming from but the lighting is amazing, color palette is great and all zones do looks alive. Keep in mind that the game also does feature Ray Tracing so I played with that setting enabled at all times which did make a difference in the visual aspect for the better. However.. optimization isn't Kuro's strongest suit as the game in some areas is very heavily single core bound that the FPS can drop below 60 in some cases. This is especially bad in the current 3.x zones. while the game is demanding, I have experienced Cyberpunk with Path Tracing looking and running far more consistent. I personally didn't have crashes with the game like a lot of others reporting but I can see from where they are coming. as WuWa does a major issue with memory management... I had sessions of playing the game for around 6 hours and the process of the game was consuming around 20GB of RAM. This in fact proves to be a major problem for those on 32GB RAM and below and expecting everyone to have 64 or more is unreasonable. The soundtrack is another strong area of the game and I feel that alone does add a lot of value to the game. be it through background music for areas or also entire songs to present different characters. There is no limitation to a specific genre too https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xFXDxA-Pe3c https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=q7i99IN7wMQ https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=b3AG_ZZlhLg and I can keep on going but I wanted to use those as an example as they were my favorites. Has Kuro took over Riot's music department? :D But to end the review, WuWa is personally one of my favorite games so far and has provided a great experience that will rival most AAA games and beat them too. 9.5/10

text: Wuthering Waves... man this game didnt just become a favorite it took over that throne forcefully. And it keeps on shining! Wuwa is my first anime-style game and now Im ruined because HOW is anything supposed to top this? Impossible! From the very first moments the story is just wow. The kind that pulls you in without asking permission. Between the intensity of dark tales with sheeps, the gladiators, the mystery of the Black Shores, and even the random fun little events (yes, Im talking about those card games I got way too invested in), everything feels crafted with so much care! You just feel the love in the details. It shines through in the world, the mechanics, the characters and the conversations all of it. And somehow, even after 221 hours (yes, I checked, and no, Im not done), I still feel like Ive barely scratched the surface. The latest area Laha Roi feels like a sweet sci-fi fantasy merged with Avatar The little Soliskin creatures? I cannot. Every time I find one, I feel like Ive been dropped into Avatar and my only purpose in life is to gently shepherd them to safety. Its equal parts magical and slightly unhinged to think about, and I love every insane second of it. I blame the devs for getting emotionally attached to every single creature. Also the story doesnt just look pretty it actually means something. Lines like: A heart isn't shaped by what it's born with or what it's been given... or They belong to no Gods but their own... -> Like EXCUSE ME? CHILLS. Theres this constant theme of light and shadow, hope and struggle, and somehow it all just sticks with you with the characters. If I had one or two complaints, its that some older characters have basically vanished from banners. There are favorites I missed and havent seen in forever, and it hurts a little. But Im holding onto hope that the anniversary banners bring them back. Also the game optimization, many still struggle with that. At the end of the day, Wuthering Waves isnt just a game I play I live here now, rent free, no plans of leaving. :D So if you want to invest your emotion somewhere, believe me, this is the place. Just be aware that you might get emotionally attached to everything and blame the devs for crying over a robot that comes out of nowhere from a minor side quest with a bucket list. Yeah I will remember you Robbie btw. <3

text: After playing Genshin for years and Honkai for a while from Hoyo I can say Wuthering Waves is a damn good competitor to them and in most places... better! They share the same world type with open ended exploration with chests littered around and puzzles to solve which i like but what sets it apart from Genshin is the movement. Movement is basically wall running and gliding around at full speed and out of combat you use no stamina which is a god send COmbat is where the game truly shines. Its significantly faster and far more skill driven than Genshin. Parrying, precise dodging, aerial combos, and quick character swaps create flashy, high impact fights that actually reward timing and awareness. Its not just button mashing it feels dynamic and satisfying The story so far is solid (with 3.1 being a unforgettable story quest). It leans into anime tropes with friendship, hope, emotional character arcs. So if thats not your thing, it might not land. But the character quests in particular are standout moments with some genuinely heartfelt scenes And yes, its a gacha. But its a generous one. THe game dosent push for you buy anything and is quite a nice experience for a F2P person compared to other Gacha games

label: positive
text: One of the rare gacha games that actually respects every player, F2P or not. TL;DR : Overall, the game is solid and if you enjoy high-action anime games with attractive characters and strong action, go for it. If you don't enjoy the gacha/grind aspect of the game, don't waste your time. 8.5/10 The game has a decent story, with amazing characters that makes you want to pull on every single banner just because the character is pretty or badass. Combat is where it shines the best, prioritizing combos and synergy to take on tougher challenges. The game offers a fun feature similar to pokemons, where you can use bosses/ennemies you find in the game as a 'special attack' to grant bonuses to your characters. Sure, it's fairly limited, and no you're not literally summoning the boss but rather a part of it (Mostly its weapons, or a specific moves it does) but it remains a neat feature that actually feel useful. The devs actually listen to players, quickly adding QoL features such as changing the gender of your character whenever you feel like it, which might sound obvious but big names like Genshin/Honkai/ZZZ, games that are older than WUWA but still lack in those tiny features. And for those interested in what 'tiny obvious features' WUWA has and that Genshin doesn't, here's a short list: - Sprinting costs no stamina outside of combat - Climbing walls/cliffs is much more faster - Traversal is easier, especially with the wings unlock. - No weekly rotation for materials As for the designs of the world and the characters, expect a much darker and serious tone than Genshin, where Genshin shines on its vivid and bright colors whilst WUWA is much more 'gray and rainy'. Like any other high budget gacha games, the game is a goonfest. 90% of new characters are attractive females, with little to no males. The entire joke around 'Wuthering Waves being Gathering Wives' is completely real. But at least all of the clearly fan-service characters are visibly adult and mature, unlike Genshin where a lot of its characters have a childish look to it, making fan service weird. Sure, the game is very good, but it also has some downsides. The main downsides that lots of players will probably agree on is the entire 'Energy-based' farming. Like other gacha games, WUWA follows the system of having limited energy to farm for ressources, items, world drops, forcing you to regularly log in and play if you want to stay on a fair level of components should a good character release. Also, making you wait a whole week for the next weekly reset to keep farming bosses is also rather annoying, but I suppose that it's to keep players committed.

label: positive
text: Wuthering Waves is a gacha game, but that doesnt automatically mean you need to throw money at it to enjoy yourself. Of course, you can spend if you want to, but its not required to progress or have fun. Personally, I only spent a couple of euros on a pass, and after 50 hours, I feel perfectly satisfied with the value Ive gotten. The game offers a decent story and a ton of content depending on what kind of player you are: Want to quest and dive into the storylines of different characters? You can. Prefer to chill, gather collectibles, and push each map to 100%? Absolutely. Into min-maxing and clearing trials as efficiently as possible? Thats there too. On top of that, the game also supports co-op mode so you can share the experience with friends, or you can play solo at your own pace. The most important thing is: dont rush. Take your time, enjoy the world, and explore everything the game has to offerbecause theres a lot here.

text: Wuthering Waves really surprised me, to be honest. I was looking for something different to play and thought Id give WuWa a shot since its free. After doing some research, I saw that the gameplay was actually pretty decent: classic RPG mechanics like buffs, items that boost crit rate or crit damage, % attack, and so on, plus a combat system with a fair amount of combos. Nothing groundbreaking. But once I started playing, I was blown away. The gameplay was exactly what I expected, but the fight animations, cutscenes, dialogue, the Echo system (which feels almost like Pokmon), and the sheer amount of quests and content this game has its insane that its free. Of course, it monetizes through gacha, but it honestly doesnt feel mandatory at all. The game throws a ton of pulls at you for free. As of right now, I have 17 characters, including seven 5-stars, six 5-star weapons, and a lot of 4-star ones, all without spending a single cent. That said, it does matter in the endgame, where youll definitely need an almost perfect team to complete the harder challenges. The game also looks incredible. The devs clearly put a lot of effort into the graphics, and it runs great too. With every patch, they optimize the game and add new details to the ever growing world of Solaris. All in all, I highly recommend it to anyone looking for an open-world game packed with activities and a surprisingly enjoyable story. Shorekeeper is still the best girl, and the best healer and buffer!

text: Very F2P friendly. Story is unmatched compared to other Gacha's in contention. If you're a fan of the Story and Lore of Kingdom Hearts or Final Fantasy(especially FFX), you will catch on pretty easily. Combat is satisfying, and certain characters reward you for playing optimally with cool animations(Shorekeeper and Lupa come to mind). Lots of exploration across 3 major regions so far. There is a grind to maximizing your characters' potential, but i'd argue that is up to your discretion at a certain point. I like the game so much I will gladly address the elephants in the room. Early Story is rough. I do understand they have to set the scene of the story, but it's a sludge until 1.3(Black Shores). The Story gets better with every patch, I guarantee it. Coop is not ideal. Performance in coop is not the issue, it's just that there's not many things to do. If you're new, friends can join you and help you beat up hard enemies, even have some fun clearing the map, or helping you find loot, but once your in the late-game, there's no incentive to further do it. We got some opportunities in 2.0 and 2.1, and they haven't really put anything in permanently to fix this. Optimization. For the first patch or two of a new area being unlocked, I tend to have huge lag spikes (I have an i7 with an RTX 3060), This was really bad in 2.4 with Septimont, but they have since fixed this. I have faith they will remedy this, but I can't let it go unspoken. If anyone is newer to the game and has some questions about making the most out of a F2P experience, I would be more than happy to give some pointers and such. Just leave a comment. This game is a lot of fun. The endgame is very cool, and these developers are very open to feedback. They've earned my trust, which I can't even say for game's I played before.

text: Wuthering Waves continues to prove itself as one of the most ambitious action RPGs on the market, and the 2.8 update is a shining example of how well the game is evolving. text: I genuinely enjoy Wuthering Waves. The combat, exploration, and overall experience on Linux via Proton was on par with Windows for me: smooth performance, no meaningful differences in gameplay. It worked, and it worked well. That changed with the 3.4 update. ACE (AntiCheat Expert) now actively blocks Linux environments, including desktop distros that previously ran the game without issues. Environment variables like STEAMDECK=1 or STEAMOS=1, which served as a workaround, no longer have any effect. The anticheat kicks the player out seconds after loading into the game. To be clear, this is not a Proton regression or a hardware issue. The game ran flawlessly before this update on the same system. The block is a deliberate policy decision on ACE's side, and Kuro Games has the ability to whitelist Linux/SteamOS in the anticheat configuration. Kuro has acknowledged Steam Deck compatibility as something they're working on, which is appreciated. But desktop Linux users who played without issues for months are now locked out with no timeline or communication. Changing to positive the moment Linux support is restored. The game itself deserves it. Edit: DWProton 11.0-3 (via ProtonPlus) works as a workaround. Still a regression from pre-3.4 behavior.
